ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Chicken soup) is the closest parent; no specific code for Kozhi Milagu Saaru exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kozhi Milagu Saaru or South Indian pepper chicken soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Kozhi Milagu Saaru is approximately 300 g, typically served as a soup course or light meal alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken soup, home-prepared — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken broth and black pepper for micronutrient estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for chicken curry and chicken soup preparations. Kozhi Milagu Saaru is a thin pepper-spiced chicken broth; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN chicken soup/broth entry (approximately 60–70 kcal/100 g, protein ~6 g, fat ~2.5 g, carbohydrate ~2 g) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy item 'Soup, chicken broth, homemade' (USDA FDC ID 173410) for micronutrient detail. The dish is predominantly water-based with chicken pieces and whole spices; macros reflect a moderately lean broth.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chicken soup and analogous low-carbohydrate meat broths — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kozhi Milagu Saaru. The University of Sydney GI Database lists chicken soup and similar low-carbohydrate meat broths as having a GI of approximately 30 (low), consistent with their very low carbohydrate content (~2 g/100 g). GI of 30 is used as the analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 2.1 × 3) / 100 = 1.89, rounded to 1.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~930 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size carefully, as a single 300 g bowl provides approximately 930 mg sodium (~40% of the 2300 mg daily limit); requesting reduced-salt preparation or consuming a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.1 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kozhi Milagu Saaru has a very low glycemic index (~30) and minimal carbohydrate load (~6.3 g per 300 g serving), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sodium intake is managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium level and saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the saturated fat content is relatively low (~0.8 g/100 g) and the dish provides lean protein, the sodium content (~930 mg per serving) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; low-sodium preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kozhi Milagu Saaru is a traditional South Indian chicken pepper soup originating from Tamil Nadu. The name derives from Tamil: 'kozhi' (chicken), 'milagu' (black pepper), and 'saaru' (broth or essence). It is prepared by simmering chicken pieces with whole and ground black pepper, turmeric, cumin, coriander seeds, garlic, ginger, curry leaves, onion, and tomato in water to produce a thin, aromatic, deeply spiced broth. The soup is characterised by a bold peppery heat, earthy spice notes, and a light golden-brown colour. It is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is commonly consumed as a restorative dish during illness or cold weather. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, high-protein, low-carbohydrate soup (~62 kcal/100 g, ~6.2 g protein, ~2.8 g fat, ~2.1 g carbohydrate) with a low glycemic index (~30), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate (~310 mg/100 g) and should be monitored by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Black pepper contributes piperine, which has been studied for its anti-inflammatory and bioavailability-enhancing properties. The dish is served in Indian restaurants in Singapore, including South Indian establishments, and is culturally significant as a traditional home remedy and festive soup.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Kozhi Milagu Saaru exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken soup/broth entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy chicken broth data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for low-carbohydrate chicken soups. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) chicken soup/broth entry and USDA SR Legacy 'Soup, chicken broth, homemade' (FDC ID 173410); no exact database entry for Kozhi Milagu Saaru was found in approved sources. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source entries for chicken broth. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source; USDA SR Legacy does not provide a reliable value for this preparation. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero) — chicken broth preparations of this type contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy reference. 6. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for low-carbohydrate chicken soups and meat broths; no direct GI measurement for Kozhi Milagu Saaru was found. 7. Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. 8. The dish is served at Manam - Upper Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 9.00 per serving. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact ontology entry exists for this specific dish.
